source.definition: To have the ability or possibility to do something.

explanation.sourceText: The English modal verb "can" originates from Old English 'cunnan', which derives from the Proto-Germanic root *kunnaną meaning 'to know, to be able'.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul englezesc „can” provine din engleza veche 'cunnan', derivat din rădăcina proto-germanică *kunnaną care înseamnă 'a ști, a putea'.

target.definition: A avea capacitatea sau posibilitatea de a face ceva.

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian verb „a putea” derives from the Latin verb 'potere', which meant 'to be able' or 'to have power'.
explanation.targetText: Verbul românesc „a putea” derivă din verbul latin 'potere', care însemna 'a putea' sau 'a fi capabil'.

target.additional: Irregular verb, conjugation similar to 'a vrea'.

examples.sourceText: Yesterday I was able to sing well.
examples.targetText: Ieri am putut să cânt bine.

examples.sourceText: I used to be able to swim better.
examples.targetText: Pe vremuri puteam să înot mai bine.

examples.sourceText: I will be able to come tomorrow.

learningTips.text: Remember that the Romanian verb 'a putea' comes from Latin 'potere', which means 'to be able'. This connection helps you link 'putea' with 'power' and ability.
The English modal verb 'can' expresses ability, just like 'a putea' in Romanian, so think of 'can' as your key to doing things.
Think of the English word 'put' to remember 'a putea'—if you can 'put' something somewhere, you have the ability to do it.
Note that 'a putea' is an irregular verb in Romanian, conjugated similarly to 'a vrea' (to want), so practice both together to remember their patterns.
